questions.question: What happens when you call `this.http.get('/api/users')` but never subscribe to the returned Observable?
options: No HTTP request is ever sent â€” `HttpClient` methods return cold Observables and nothing fires until subscription
The request is sent immediately and the response is silently discarded
The request is sent once on the next change-detection cycle
Angular throws a runtime error because the Observable was created but not consumed
questions.explanation: `HttpClient` methods return cold Observables: no request is issued until something subscribes. With no subscriber, the request never fires (and there is no error).

questions.question: In modern Angular, what is the role of `withFetch()` when calling `provideHttpClient(...)`?
options: It is deprecated and unnecessary â€” the Fetch API is now the default `HttpBackend`
It is still required to enable the fetch backend, which is otherwise off by default
It switches the backend from fetch back to `XMLHttpRequest`
It enables upload progress reporting that fetch otherwise cannot provide
questions.explanation: Fetch became the default backend in v22, so `withFetch()` is now deprecated and unnecessary. `withXhr()` is the explicit opt-out, and it is XHR (not fetch) that reports upload progress.

questions.question: Calling `http.get<User[]>(url)` makes Angular validate at runtime that the response body actually matches the `User[]` shape.
questions.explanation: The generic is a compile-time type assertion only; Angular performs no runtime payload validation. You must validate untrusted data yourself.

questions.question: If `value` contains the string `<script>alert(1)</script>` and you render `{{ value }}` in a template, what does the user see?
options: The literal text `<script>alert(1)</script>`, escaped and never executed
An empty element â€” Angular removes script tags entirely
An executed alert dialog, because interpolation does not sanitize
A sanitizer warning logged to the console with the value omitted
questions.explanation: Interpolation auto-escapes its content to text, so the markup is shown literally and never executed. It is the default and safest sink.

questions.question: Which Angular service receives uncaught exceptions thrown during change detection, lifecycle hooks, and event handlers?
options: `ErrorHandler`
`HttpErrorResponse`
`NavigationError`
`DomSanitizer`
questions.explanation: Uncaught exceptions funnel to the single injectable `ErrorHandler` (default: log to console), which you replace app-wide to forward to a monitoring backend.

questions.question: Given `withInterceptors([authInterceptor, loggingInterceptor, errorInterceptor])`, which interceptor is closest to the backend on the way out, and which sees the response first on the way back?
options: `errorInterceptor` is closest to the backend out; `errorInterceptor` sees the response first on the way back
`authInterceptor` is closest to the backend out; `authInterceptor` sees the response first on the way back
`loggingInterceptor` is closest to the backend out; `errorInterceptor` sees the response first on the way back
`errorInterceptor` is closest to the backend out; `authInterceptor` sees the response first on the way back
questions.explanation: Interceptors run in array order outbound and reverse order inbound. The last listed (`errorInterceptor`) is closest to the backend on the way out and, being innermost, is the first to see the response coming back.

questions.question: An `HttpErrorResponse` arrives in `catchError` with `err.status === 0`. What does that indicate?
options: A network, CORS, or timeout failure â€” the request never reached or never got a usable response from the backend
A successful request whose body was empty
A backend error such as 500 that Angular normalizes to 0
An authentication failure that should redirect to `/login`
questions.explanation: Status `0` means a network/CORS/timeout failure where no HTTP status was received. Any non-zero status is an actual backend error response.

questions.question: What is wrong with this code?
options: `HttpParams` is immutable â€” `.set()` returns a NEW instance, so the original `params` still has no values and the request sends none
`HttpParams` values must be numbers, so passing strings throws at runtime
You cannot call `.set()` twice on the same `HttpParams`; the second call overwrites and erases `active`
`params` must be passed via `withInterceptors`, not the request options object
questions.explanation: `HttpParams` is immutable: `.set()` returns a new instance and does not mutate in place. Because the returned instances are discarded, the original `params` is still empty when passed to `get`.

questions.question: You need the HTTP status code and response headers (not just the parsed body) from a `DELETE` call. Which option do you pass?
options: `{ observe: 'response' }` to receive the full `HttpResponse<T>`
`{ observe: 'body' }`, which is the default and includes headers
`{ responseType: 'json' }` so headers are merged into the body
`{ observe: 'events' }`, the only way to read a status code
questions.explanation: `observe: 'response'` yields the full `HttpResponse<T>` with status and headers. `'body'` (the default) gives only the parsed body; `'events'` is for progress streams.

questions.question: A property holds `'hello'`. It is bound with `[innerHTML]="value"`. What renders?
options: The image element and the text `hello`, with the `onerror` handler stripped by the sanitizer
Nothing â€” the sanitizer rejects the entire string because it contains an event handler
The image plus a working `onerror` handler, since `[innerHTML]` does not sanitize
The literal escaped text `hello`
questions.explanation: `[innerHTML]` keeps sanitized markup but drops scripts and event handlers, so the `onerror` is removed while the element and text remain. Interpolation would have escaped the whole string instead.

questions.question: How does `HttpClient`'s built-in XSRF protection work by default?
options: It reads the `XSRF-TOKEN` cookie and echoes it in the `X-XSRF-TOKEN` header on mutating requests; the server validates the header
It generates a random token client-side and sends it in both the cookie and the header so the server can compare them
It encrypts every request body with a token derived from the user's session
It blocks all cross-origin requests unless the server returns a matching CORS header
questions.explanation: Angular reads the server-set `XSRF-TOKEN` cookie and echoes its value in the `X-XSRF-TOKEN` header on mutating requests. The server sets the cookie and validates the header; names are tunable via `withXsrfConfiguration`.

questions.question: Inside a functional `HttpInterceptorFn`, you can call `inject(AuthService)` directly because the interceptor runs in the injection context of its registrar.
questions.explanation: Functional interceptors execute in the injection context where they were registered, so `inject()` works inside the function body â€” as the `authInterceptor` does to read the token.

questions.question: Which statements about `httpResource()` are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: It re-fetches automatically when a signal read inside its reactive URL function changes
It still flows through your registered HTTP interceptors
It is GET-oriented for declarative reads; use `HttpClient` directly for `POST`/`PUT`/`DELETE` mutations
It returns a cold Observable that you must `subscribe` to before any request fires
It bypasses sanitization, so its results are inserted into the DOM as trusted
questions.explanation: `httpResource()` is a signal-native wrapper: it re-fetches when read signals change, still passes through interceptors, and is GET-oriented (mutations use `HttpClient`). It exposes signals like `value()`/`isLoading()` rather than a cold Observable, and it has nothing to do with bypassing DOM sanitization.

questions.question: You bind a dynamic URL to `<iframe [src]="url">`. How does Angular treat the `RESOURCE_URL` security context here, compared to binding a URL to `[href]`?
options: `RESOURCE_URL` is not auto-sanitized â€” you must explicitly mark the value trusted (e.g. `bypassSecurityTrustResourceUrl`), whereas `[href]` (URL context) is auto-sanitized
Both are auto-sanitized identically; the iframe simply blocks `javascript:` URLs like `[href]` does
`RESOURCE_URL` is auto-sanitized more strictly than `[href]`, requiring no developer action
Neither is sanitized; URL contexts are always trusted by Angular
questions.explanation: The `RESOURCE_URL` context (e.g. `<iframe src>`) has no auto-sanitizer because the resource is fully loaded and executed; Angular requires explicit trust. The plain URL context for `[href]`/`[src]` is auto-sanitized to block `javascript:` and unsafe URLs.

questions.question: A developer pipes user-submitted profile bios through `DomSanitizer.bypassSecurityTrustHtml(...)` so users can use bold/italic markup. What is the consequence?
options: It reintroduces an XSS vulnerability â€” bypassing tells Angular to stop sanitizing, so attacker-supplied scripts and handlers reach the DOM intact
It is safe, because `bypassSecurityTrustHtml` still strips scripts and event handlers before trusting the value
It only affects styling and cannot execute scripts, so there is no security impact
Angular ignores the bypass for values that originated from user input and sanitizes anyway
questions.explanation: `bypassSecurityTrust*` marks a value as trusted so Angular stops sanitizing it entirely. Applying it to user input lets malicious scripts and handlers through â€” bypass only values you fully control.

questions.question: An unhandled promise rejection occurs in code running outside Angular's call stack. Which provider ensures it still reaches your custom `ErrorHandler`?
options: `provideBrowserGlobalErrorListeners()`, which routes `window` `error` and `unhandledrejection` events into `ErrorHandler`
`provideHttpClient(withInterceptors([...]))`, since interceptors catch all async errors
`withNavigationErrorHandler(fn)`, which forwards every async failure to the router
`provideBrowserGlobalErrorListeners()` is unnecessary because `ErrorHandler` already captures all `window`-level events
questions.explanation: By default `ErrorHandler` only sees exceptions in Angular's own call stack. `provideBrowserGlobalErrorListeners()` wires `window` `error` and `unhandledrejection` events into the same `ErrorHandler`, catching out-of-stack failures.

questions.question: Given this error interceptor, what happens to a request that fails 3 times in a row with status 500?
options: It is retried twice (3 attempts total); after the final failure `catchError` maps it to a new `Error` that is re-thrown to the subscriber
It is retried indefinitely until it succeeds, because `catchError` resubscribes
`catchError` swallows the error and the Observable completes silently with no value
The `retry` and `catchError` cancel each other out, so the original `HttpErrorResponse` reaches the subscriber unchanged
questions.explanation: `retry({ count: 2 })` makes up to 3 attempts; once they are exhausted the error propagates to `catchError`, which maps it to a new `Error` and re-throws via `throwError`, so the subscriber still receives a (cleaner) error rather than a swallowed one.

questions.question: Which situations are appropriately routed through the central `ErrorHandler`? (Select all that apply.)
options: An uncaught exception thrown inside a component lifecycle hook
An uncaught exception thrown in a template event handler
A route guard returning `false` to block navigation
A form validation failure surfaced as an invalid control
An error you already handled with a `try/catch` and recovered from
questions.explanation: `ErrorHandler` is for uncaught exceptions during change detection, lifecycle hooks, and event handlers. A guard returning `false`, a validation failure, and already-caught errors are ordinary control flow or expected outcomes â€” not exceptions to route through `ErrorHandler`.
